license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: RD Gateway service started
entries.summary: The Remote Desktop Gateway service started successfully.
entries.details: Generated when the Remote Desktop Gateway service starts. This event confirms the RD Gateway is operational. From a security monitoring perspective, unexpected restarts of the RD Gateway service — particularly outside maintenance windows or following configuration changes — may indicate tampering with the gateway infrastructure or a response to an attack. Correlate with System log EID 7036 (service state change) for the same service and with any preceding errors or configuration change events.

entries.title: RD Gateway connection authorized (CAP passed)
entries.summary: A user met the connection authorization policy and was permitted to connect through the RD Gateway.
entries.details: Generated when a user successfully passes the Connection Authorization Policy (CAP) evaluation at the RD Gateway. CAP controls who is allowed to connect through the gateway (authentication, group membership, device health). Key fields: Username, ClientAddress (external source IP), and the CAP name that was satisfied. This event represents successful external authentication to the RD Gateway and is the primary event for baselining who is accessing the gateway. Every EID 200 event should be expected — unexpected usernames, off-hours connections, or connections from unusual geographic locations or IP ranges warrant investigation. Correlate with EID 302 to confirm the user also reached an internal resource.

investigationPivots: Compare ClientAddress against known VPN egress IPs, corporate office IPs, and user's historical connection locations
Off-hours connections (outside business hours) from unfamiliar IPs for privileged accounts warrant immediate review
High volume of EID 200 events from a single ClientAddress across multiple usernames may indicate credential stuffing success
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate remote workers connecting from home or travel — ClientAddress will vary

entries.title: RD Gateway connection denied (CAP failed)
entries.summary: A user failed the connection authorization policy and was denied access through the RD Gateway.
entries.details: Generated when a user fails the Connection Authorization Policy evaluation at the RD Gateway. CAP failure can occur due to authentication failure (wrong credentials), lack of group membership in the allowed user group, or device health policy failure. Key fields: Username, ClientAddress (external source IP), and the reason for denial. This is the primary event for detecting brute-force and credential stuffing attacks against the RD Gateway from the internet. High volumes of EID 201 events from a single ClientAddress or targeting a single Username indicate an active attack. Unlike Security log EID 4625 (which logs failed logons on the internal host), EID 201 is logged on the gateway itself — visible before authentication reaches any internal system.

investigationPivots: Count EID 201 events per ClientAddress per hour — thresholds above 10-20 per hour from one IP indicate automated spraying
Multiple usernames targeted from a single ClientAddress = credential spray; single username from multiple IPs = distributed brute-force
A sequence of EID 201 events followed by a single EID 200 from the same ClientAddress confirms a successful attack after failures
commonFalsePositives: Users with expired passwords or recently changed passwords attempting to connect before updating credentials
Misconfigured RD Gateway client software sending incorrect authentication material

entries.title: RD Gateway connection denied (NAP health policy failed)
entries.summary: A user was denied access through the RD Gateway because their device failed the Network Access Protection health policy.
entries.details: Generated when a user passes authentication but is denied access because their device fails the Network Access Protection (NAP) health policy check configured at the RD Gateway. NAP checks can enforce requirements such as firewall status, AV signature currency, OS patch level, or custom health validators. Key fields: Username, ClientAddress, and the health policy that failed. NAP failures can indicate a user is connecting from an unmanaged or out-of-compliance device. From a security perspective, this event can also flag compromised devices that have had security controls (AV, firewall) disabled — an attacker accessing a victim account from their own device will fail NAP checks enforced by the organization.

investigationPivots: Identify the health policy that failed — AV disabled or firewall off on a remote device is a significant indicator
Correlate the Username with the device the user normally connects from — a NAP failure from a new device may indicate account compromise
Repeated NAP failures followed by a NAP success from the same ClientAddress may indicate policy bypass attempts
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate users connecting from personal or unmanaged devices not enrolled in MDM
Devices with temporarily expired AV signatures or pending Windows updates

entries.title: RD Gateway resource access denied (RAP failed)
entries.summary: A user passed connection authorization but was denied access to the requested internal resource by the resource authorization policy.
entries.details: Generated when a user passes the CAP (connection authorization) but is denied access to a specific internal resource by the Resource Authorization Policy (RAP). RAP controls which internal hosts a user is permitted to reach through the gateway — limiting lateral movement potential even for authenticated users. Key fields: Username, ClientAddress, and the internal resource (TargetServer) that was denied. EID 301 is significant because it logs the specific internal host the user attempted to reach, making it a reconnaissance and lateral movement detection event. An attacker who has compromised an account and authenticated through the gateway (EID 200) may then attempt to reach internal hosts beyond their authorized scope, generating EID 301.

investigationPivots: TargetServer reveals which internal host was probed — attempts to reach domain controllers, file servers, or security tools by non-privileged accounts are critical
A sequence of EID 200 (CAP pass) → multiple EID 301 (RAP denies to different hosts) from the same session indicates internal reconnaissance via the gateway
Compare TargetServer against the user's role — accessing systems outside their normal work scope warrants review
commonFalsePositives: Users who have legitimate access to a system but the RAP was not updated to include their new access requirement
IT administrators testing connectivity to new systems before RAP policies are updated

entries.title: RD Gateway resource connection succeeded (RAP passed)
entries.summary: A user passed the resource authorization policy and successfully connected to an internal resource through the RD Gateway.
entries.details: Generated when a user passes both the CAP (EID 200) and the RAP, and successfully establishes a connection to an internal resource through the gateway. This is the end-to-end success event: the user authenticated, was authorized by connection policy, authorized by resource policy, and reached their destination host. Key fields: Username, ClientAddress (external source IP), and TargetServer (the internal host reached). This event pair — EID 200 + EID 302 — is the primary baseline for RD Gateway activity. Every session should produce both. Anomalies: unexpected TargetServer values, connections outside business hours, or new ClientAddress IPs for a user all warrant investigation.

investigationPivots: TargetServer identifies which internal host was accessed — combine with TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager EID 21 on the target host to confirm session establishment
Build a baseline of {Username → TargetServer} pairs; connections to new TargetServers for an account indicate scope expansion
ClientAddress pivot: the same external IP connecting as multiple different users is a strong credential sharing or compromise indicator
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate remote work generating connections outside normal business hours
IT staff accessing multiple servers through the gateway during incident response
